channel.title: Daily Feed (Last 48 Hours, Updated 9 AM BD)
channel.description: Daily Feed (Last 48 Hours, Updated 9 AM BD) generated by script

item.title: The Geopolitics of SpaceX and Elon Musk
item.description: Should one company—and one person—have this much power?

item.title: Restocking the Pentagon
item.description: Trump urges defense manufacturers to speed up weapons production after the Iran war.

item.title: Will Kenya’s Ruto Finally Reconcile with the Country’s Somali Minority?
item.description: The president has taken unprecedented steps to end a policy of exclusion, but suspicions remain.

item.title: As the Tide Turns Against Putin, Beware the Drowning Man
item.description: Like a struggling swimmer, he may take desperate measures to stay afloat.

item.title: Venezuela suffers its worst earthquake in a century
item.description: The impoverished country is struggling to cope with the damage from two nearly simultaneous shocks

item.title: Venezuela Reels From Twin Deadly Earthquakes
item.description: With thousands of people missing, the death toll is expected to rise.

item.title: Where will Europe’s heatwave be most deadly?
item.description: We analyse 854 cities to see which will be worst affected

item.title: EU-Kazakhstan: Connectivity at the Core
item.description: Amid Kazakhstan and the European Union's multifaceted relations, connectivity remains a throughline.

item.title: India’s Critical Mineral Challenge in a New Global Resource Order
item.description: Mineral security is not just about access to resources but about control over value chains. India’s response must be strategic, not reactive.

item.title: The Political Risks Facing Washington’s New Ambassador to South Korea
item.description: Michelle Steel arrives in Seoul at a moment when ethnic affinity and political polarization make diplomacy more complicated than ever.

item.title: The Mask of Engagement: How Fragmented International Interests Normalize Gender Apartheid in Afghanistan
item.description: The continuous compromise of human rights standards by bodies like the United Nations provides the Taliban with the precise economic, political, and psychological lifelines required to sustain their autocracy.

item.title: The Quest for Nuclear Superiority Makes No Sense
item.description: The best way for governments to save money is to stop wasting it on completely unnecessary weapons.

item.title: Amid a Slowing Economy, China Is Changing How It Judges Local Party Leaders
item.description: Longstanding metrics – particularly rapid GDP growth and infrastructural expansion – are no longer sufficient to sustain political legitimacy.

item.title: Australia’s Emerging Role in Climate-Tech Governance
item.description: Melbourne has joined the Global Urban Data Centers Pact, to tackle the nexus between new technologies and climate change.

item.title: The Deepening of Australia-Indonesia Ties Yields Dividends With Fertilizer Shipment
item.description: Tonnes of fertilizer are being unloaded in Brisbane for Australian farmers. It’s not a one-off deal but the product of consistent fertilizing of the relationship between Canberra and Jakarta.

item.title: AI models’ values are very different from most people’s
item.description: They are more secular and more liberal—unless they’re made in China

item.title: Latin America has turned Trumpy. That creates opportunities
item.description: Never before have the politics of the region shifted so quickly

item.title: Why philosophy is having a moment
item.description: A world of clever machines makes it newly relevant

item.title: University-for-all harms poor students the most
item.description: Time for sane standards in higher education

item.title: With Iran emboldened, its neighbours must put old divisions aside
item.description: With a common foe, they cannot afford petty feuds

item.title: Two state elections may break Malaysia’s ruling coalition
item.description: Anwar Ibrahim’s long-sought tenure as prime minister may be cut short

item.title: Asian governments are making children care for their parents
item.description: As populations age, the elderly are being neglected

item.title: Bangladesh’s main industry is battered by blackouts and rising costs
item.description: A crisis may loom

item.title: Students are doing worse than you think
item.description: Some at college or university are testing no better than ten-year-olds

item.title: J.D. Vance is heir to a more radical politics than Trumpism
item.description: …if, of course, he believes what he’s saying

item.title: America’s pro-Israel lobby considers next steps
item.description: Israel’s defenders are flailing at the White House and at the ballot box

item.title: The bipartisan backlash against AI in America
item.description: About the only thing that unites Democrats and Republicans is fear of artificial intelligence

item.title: Business
Politics
The EU is just too damn slow
item.description: Why Brussels needs to pick up the pace

item.title: France’s south is testing out hard-right rule
item.description: The National Rally is mixing raw nativism with promises of fiscal liberalism

item.title: Why Germany’s cities are going broke
item.description: Bad news for residents, good news for the far right’s electoral chances

item.title: Peter Magyar is trying to fire up Hungarian democracy
item.description: A constitutional amendment would burn away the remnants of Viktor Orban’s regime

item.title: Teaching AI how people work is fraught with problems
item.description: Tacit knowledge is vital to many jobs

item.title: Why easyJet may be heading for a break-up
item.description: Its private-equity suitor may be looking to sell it for parts

item.title: UPS is losing ground to FedEx
item.description: The two logistics giants have similar strategies—and face a common threat

item.title: Medieval-style fortifications are back in the Sahel
item.description: Newly walled towns are a sign of shrivelling state authority

item.title: Does Gadi Eisenkot offer Israel a new chance?
item.description: Israelis think he may be a genuine alternative to Binyamin Netanyahu

item.title: Ebola has put Africa’s fragile health systems in the spotlight
item.description: Emergencies and aid cuts are accelerating a push for self-reliance

item.title: What a surge of anti-migrant protests says about South Africa
item.description: It is too easy for xenophobic vigilantes to take advantage of poverty and a weak state

item.title: America is using force to drive gangsters from Venezuela’s gold
item.description: It won’t be a pushover

item.title: Two powerful earthquakes hit Venezuela
item.description: Such tightly sequenced quakes are rare

item.title: The dramatic Trumpification of Latin America
item.description: Real problems, copycattery and a network of hacks have helped Donald Trump’s pals win a string of elections

item.title: Democrats and Republicans agree: AI is scary
item.description: It’s about the only thing that unites them

item.title: Vietnam’s National Champions
item.description: Risks and opportunities for foreign investors

item.title: Building a Battery Champion: What CATL and Dongqiao Reveal About China’s Industrial Model
item.description: China’s dominant role in electric vehicle batteries owes much to local government policy.

item.title: Cartoon: Russia’s war fatigue grows
The Tide Is Turning Against Russia
item.description: With Ukraine reclaiming territory, mounting drone strikes deep in Russian territory, and draining Russia’s coffers, Vladimir Putin would do well to seek a negotiated freeze to the conflict. But there is no guarantee that he will, not least because he might not grasp the situation Russia is in.

item.title: Managing Sino-American Interdependence
item.description: At their summit last month, US President Donald Trump and Chinese President Xi Jinping seemed to recognize that they are in the same boat. If either the United States or China tries to cripple or decouple from the other, it will harm its own firms, consumers, financial markets, and innovation ecosystem.

item.title: The US in Brief: The Sharpie cap stays on
item.description: Our daily political update, featuring the stories that matter

item.title: Who Gains in an AI-Supercharged Economy?
item.description: If AI delivers as promised and fundamentally transforms the economy, we will have moved from the current investment phase, where AI builders dominate, to one where AI users will reap the greatest reward. That is what happens every time an innovation becomes a general-purpose technology.

item.title: Global imbalances have little to do with Europe’s industrial woes
item.description: The EU has forgotten that it, like China, is a surplus economy

item.title: Will AI lower interest rates?
item.description: Kevin Warsh is drawing lessons about tech from Alan Greenspan—but selectively

item.title: Both Donald Trump and Giorgia Meloni are begging for trouble
item.description: The dalliance between the populist-right leaders of America and Italy is off—or is it?

item.title: Andy Burnham promises hope. Britain needs more than that
item.description: He shows little sign that he grasps the scale or urgency of the tasks that await him

item.title: Development in a Time of Disruption
item.description: Once firms and economies ascend to market dominance, they often kick away the ladder behind them and erect barriers to market entry to stymie would-be challengers. But developing countries can and do leapfrog incumbent technologies, and conditions for doing so may be particularly favorable now.

item.title: The AI backlash is only getting started
item.description: Here is how to deal with it

item.title: The Gulf’s three post-war challenges
item.description: Geography, cash and confidence will decide who recovers, and who drifts

item.title: Putin and Anwar’s US Dollar Swap Deal
item.description: Russia's assurance that it will maintain a long-term supply of oil and gas to Malaysia looks like a veiled effort to keep Putin's government stocked with U.S. dollars.

item.title: Free Theary Seng: The US Should Not Forget About Cambodia’s Political Prisoners
item.description: The 55-year-old civil society activist and dual Cambodian-U.S. citizen has been locked up on spurious charges since 2022. She is just one of many.

item.title: How New Delhi’s Policy on Ceasefires With Rebel Groups in the Northeast Contributed to Manipur’s Crisis
item.description: Abrogation of the ceasefires in Manipur may not be an option for the government as it could spark greater violence.

item.title: This Industrial Revolution Is Not Like the Last One
item.description: Policymakers’ approach to automation won’t work for AI.

item.title: Why Latin America’s New Right Will Struggle to Govern
item.description: In Colombia and elsewhere, winning was the easy part.

item.title: Iran Didn’t Win the War
item.description: Tehran is still losing the long game.

item.title: A Peacemaker Tries to Defuse Tribal Nastiness of the Thai-Cambodian Border Conflict
item.description: Hyper-nationalistic rhetoric is complicating the resolution of the ongoing border dispute, but ending it is not an easy task.

item.title: US Government Imposes Fresh Sanctions Against Alleged Cambodian Scam Network
item.description: The Treasury Department has designated nine people and 26 ‌entities linked to Prince Holding Group, which it has described as a "transnational criminal organization."

item.title: MSCI Extends Review on Indonesia ‘Emerging Market’ Status Review
item.description: The reprieve led to a brief rally on the Indonesian stock market, but the longer-term outlook is less certain.

item.title: A Taiwan Crisis and America’s Industrial Base
item.description: The cost of offshoring would be laid bare in a crisis.

item.title: How the Russia-Ukraine War Rewired Southeast Asia’s Arms Trade
item.description: The war did what years of Western pressure could not, effectively removing Russia as a viable arms supplier to Southeast Asia.

item.title: Do high-tech “add-ons” increase the chance that IVF will work?
item.description: A big new review suggests most such procedures don’t help

item.title: The Supreme Court is limiting plaintiffs’ ability to hold the powerful to account
item.description: Two rulings mark a subtle but important shift
